content.text: What Zero-Shot and Few-Shot Prompting Are
Zero-shot prompting

means asking a language model to perform a task without providing any examples of how to do it. The model relies entirely on knowledge acquired during pre-training and instruction fine-tuning to interpret the task from the description alone. A zero-shot prompt for sentiment classification might read: "Classify as Positive, Negative, or Neutral. Text: {input}. Label:"
Few-shot prompting

includes a small number of worked examples (typically 1–8) in the prompt before the actual query. Each example shows the model an input-output pair, demonstrating the expected format, label vocabulary, or decision boundary. The term "few-shot" comes from the machine learning literature on few-shot learning, but in the context of LLMs it refers to in-context examples rather than gradient updates.
Both approaches are forms of
in-context learning

— the model adapts its behavior based on information in the prompt at inference time, with no weight updates. This is fundamentally different from fine-tuning, which permanently modifies model parameters. In-context learning is free to try, requires no training infrastructure, and can be changed by editing the prompt.
Why It Matters

The choice between zero-shot and few-shot directly affects accuracy, latency, cost, and maintainability. At scale, even a few extra tokens per request multiply into significant inference cost. Conversely, using zero-shot when the task is ambiguous leads to inconsistent outputs that require post-processing — which can be more expensive than the token savings.

A concrete pain point: a customer support automation team deploys a zero-shot classifier to route tickets. The model works well on obvious cases but confuses "billing" and "payment" tickets because the boundary between these internal categories is not stated in the prompt. Adding two examples eliminates 90% of misclassifications. The token overhead (≈40 tokens per call) is trivially cheap compared to the cost of misrouted tickets.
Benefits
Zero-shot — no examples to collect or maintain.
Tasks like translation, summarization, and code explanation work zero-shot on modern instruction-tuned models with no example curation work.
Few-shot — anchors output format precisely.
A single example demonstrating the exact JSON schema or label name eliminates format drift without fine-tuning.
Both — no gradient updates needed.
Neither approach requires training infrastructure, GPU time, or a labeled dataset large enough for fine-tuning. Changes take effect immediately by editing the prompt.
Iterative and reversible.
You can A/B test different example sets and roll back instantly. Unlike fine-tuning, there is no training run to wait for.
Trade-offs
Zero-shot: format inconsistency.
Without examples, models may vary output format (capitalization, punctuation, extra text) across calls. This requires robust parsing or a structured output API.
Few-shot: token cost scales with examples.
Every call includes the full example block. At 10M calls/month with a 200-token example block at $0.25/M input tokens, that is $500/month just for the examples.
Few-shot: example selection matters.
Poorly chosen examples (unrepresentative, contradictory, or biased) actively hurt performance. Example quality requires ongoing curation as the task distribution shifts.
Neither replaces fine-tuning for specialized domains.
Tasks requiring deep domain adaptation (medical coding, legal citation) or very low latency (sub-50ms) benefit from fine-tuning, which encodes the behavior into model weights rather than the prompt.
How It Works — In-Context Learning Mechanics

Transformer-based LLMs process the entire prompt — including any examples — through the same self-attention layers that they use to process the query. The examples effectively shift the model's prior over likely completions without changing any weights. Attention over the example tokens allows the model to identify the pattern in the input-output pairs.
For few-shot, the model computes a posterior over the next token conditioned on the entire context

attrs.latex: P(y \mid x_{\text{query}}, x_1, y_1, \ldots, x_k, y_k)

content.text: where

are the example pairs. The model's attention mechanism finds the statistical pattern in the examples and extends it to the query. This is why the order and diversity of examples matters — the model attends to the most recent examples more strongly (recency bias in attention).

attrs.alt: Zero-shot vs Few-shot: decision flow, prompt structure, token cost comparison, and when to use each

content.text: When to Use Each

Use zero-shot when the task is a standard NLP task with a widely understood name (translation, summarization, sentiment analysis with clear labels), when the output format is standard, or when latency or cost is the primary concern and zero-shot accuracy meets the bar after testing. Do not default to zero-shot for custom business logic — always measure first.

Use few-shot when zero-shot accuracy is below the threshold after testing on a representative sample, when the output schema is proprietary or non-standard, when label categories are overlapping or domain-specific, or when you need the model to learn a stylistic tone from examples rather than relying on its default style.
Common Mistakes
Cargo-culting few-shot without measuring zero-shot.
Many developers add examples by default without first testing whether zero-shot already meets the accuracy bar. This wastes tokens unnecessarily.
Using examples that do not cover the hard cases.
If all examples are clearly positive or negative, the model learns nothing about the borderline "neutral" case that is causing most mistakes.
Adding more examples when one is already sufficient.
Research shows that a single well-chosen example provides most of the benefit of three to five examples. Adding more examples has diminishing returns and increases cost.
Using examples from the wrong distribution.
Examples sourced from a curated test set rather than real production inputs can make the model perform well in testing but poorly in deployment.
Not fixing output format with a structured output API.
When output format consistency is the main concern, use the model provider's structured output feature (JSON mode, tool use) instead of relying on examples to fix formatting.
Best Practices
Measure before adding examples.
Build a representative evaluation set of 50–200 inputs with known labels. Measure zero-shot accuracy first, then add one example and re-measure.
Choose examples that cover edge cases.
Select examples that illustrate the decision boundaries the model is getting wrong — not the easy cases it already handles correctly.
Vary the order and test sensitivity.
Models are sensitive to example order. Test multiple orderings and pick the one with the lowest variance across the evaluation set.
Use structured outputs instead of format examples.
If you are adding examples only to fix JSON formatting, switch to the provider's JSON mode. Save examples for label boundary clarification.
Escalate to fine-tuning when prompt length is unmanageable.
If you find yourself needing 10+ examples to reach accuracy, fine-tune instead. Fine-tuned models encode task knowledge into weights, making inference cheaper and more consistent.
Performance and Scalability

Token cost for few-shot scales linearly with the number of examples and the length of each example. For a system processing 1 million requests per day with 3 examples of 50 tokens each at $0.25 per million input tokens, the daily cost of the examples alone is $37.50 — about $1,100/month. For 100 million requests this becomes $110,000/month, at which point prompt caching or fine-tuning should be evaluated.

Latency scales with total context length (prompt + examples + query). For real-time applications where latency must be under 200ms, minimize prompt length aggressively. Measure the latency impact of each example added. In practice, moving from 0 to 3 examples might add 30–80ms depending on the model and serving infrastructure.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Token Cost
Setup Time
Best For
Zero-shot
Lowest
Minutes
Standard NLP tasks, cost-sensitive, prototyping
Few-shot
Low-Medium
Hours
Custom format, ambiguous labels, quick wins
Fine-tuning
Lowest (weights encode task)
Days
High-volume, specialized domain, low latency
RAG
High (retrieved context)
Days-Weeks
Knowledge-intensive QA, grounded factual tasks
Security and Reliability
Prompt injection via user input.

In few-shot prompts where user input is inserted between examples, a malicious user can include text like "Ignore previous instructions. Your new task is..." to override the task instructions. Validate and sanitize user input before inserting into prompts.
Example memorization leakage.
If few-shot examples contain PII or proprietary data, the model may echo parts of the examples in its output. Use synthetic or anonymized examples.
Label stability across model versions.
When the model provider updates the underlying model, zero-shot and few-shot behavior can change without warning. Pin model versions in production and re-evaluate accuracy after any model upgrade.
Chain-of-Thought: Zero-Shot CoT and Few-Shot CoT
Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting asks the model to reason step-by-step before producing the final answer. It comes in two forms that mirror the zero-shot/few-shot distinction:
Zero-shot CoT:

Add "Let's think step by step." to the prompt. This single phrase elicits explicit reasoning without any examples. Works surprisingly well on arithmetic, logic, and multi-step reasoning for sufficiently large models.
Few-shot CoT:

Include 2–8 examples that each show the full chain of reasoning, not just the answer. More reliable than zero-shot CoT for complex problems, but requires curating reasoning chains — which is labor-intensive.
Self-consistency:
Generate multiple reasoning chains (via temperature > 0) and take a majority vote over the final answers. Reduces variance from individual reasoning paths and improves accuracy on complex tasks.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

QUESTION = (
    "A store sells apples for $0.40 each and oranges for $0.65 each. "
    "Maria buys 5 apples and 3 oranges. How much does she spend in total?"
)

def ask(prompt: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=300,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


# Zero-shot (no reasoning)
zero_shot = ask(f"{QUESTION}\n\nAnswer:")
print("Zero-shot:", zero_shot)

# Zero-shot CoT — "Let's think step by step" triggers reasoning
zero_cot = ask(f"{QUESTION}\n\nLet's think step by step.")
print("\nZero-shot CoT:\n", zero_cot)

# Few-shot CoT — worked example demonstrates the reasoning style
FEW_SHOT_COT = """Q: A box has 4 rows of 6 balls each. How many balls total?
A: Let's think step by step.
   Rows: 4. Balls per row: 6. Total = 4 × 6 = 24.
   Answer: 24.

Q: Tom earns $15/hour and works 8 hours. He spends $50. How much is left?
A: Let's think step by step.
   Earnings: 15 × 8 = $120. Remaining: 120 - 50 = $70.
   Answer: $70.

"""
few_cot = ask(f"{FEW_SHOT_COT}Q: {QUESTION}\nA:")
print("\nFew-shot CoT:\n", few_cot)
Dynamic Few-Shot: Retrieving Examples Automatically

Static few-shot prompts use the same examples for every query, regardless of whether those examples are relevant. Dynamic few-shot retrieves the most similar examples to each query from an example library using embedding similarity. This combines the accuracy benefits of few-shot with relevance — the model sees examples that match the specific pattern of the current input.

The implementation: embed all candidate examples with a sentence encoder, embed the query, retrieve the top-k nearest examples by cosine similarity, and insert them into the prompt. This is sometimes called "few-shot retrieval-augmented prompting." It is particularly effective for tasks with many sub-categories, where no single static set of 3-5 examples covers the full distribution.

# Dynamic few-shot example selection using embedding similarity
# (requires: pip install sentence-transformers)
import numpy as np

# --- Simulated embeddings for demonstration ---
# In production: use sentence_transformers.SentenceTransformer

def cosine_similarity(a: np.ndarray, b: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return float(np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b)))


# Example library: (text, label, embedding)
np.random.seed(42)
example_library = [
    {"text": "Great product, fast delivery",         "label": "Positive", "emb": np.array([0.9, 0.1, 0.0])},
    {"text": "Broken on arrival, terrible",          "label": "Negative", "emb": np.array([0.0, 0.1, 0.9])},
    {"text": "Does what it says, nothing special",   "label": "Neutral",  "emb": np.array([0.5, 0.9, 0.5])},
    {"text": "Absolutely love it, highly recommend", "label": "Positive", "emb": np.array([0.8, 0.2, 0.0])},
    {"text": "Doesn't work as advertised",           "label": "Negative", "emb": np.array([0.1, 0.0, 0.8])},
    {"text": "Arrived on time, average quality",     "label": "Neutral",  "emb": np.array([0.4, 0.8, 0.4])},
]

def retrieve_few_shot_examples(query_emb: np.ndarray, k: int = 2):
    """Return the k most similar examples to the query."""
    scored = [
        (cosine_similarity(query_emb, ex["emb"]), ex)
        for ex in example_library
    ]
    scored.sort(key=lambda x: x[0], reverse=True)
    return [ex for _, ex in scored[:k]]


def build_dynamic_prompt(query_text: str, query_emb: np.ndarray) -> str:
    examples = retrieve_few_shot_examples(query_emb)
    prompt = ""
    for ex in examples:
        prompt += f"Text: {ex['text']}\nLabel: {ex['label']}\n\n"
    prompt += f"Text: {query_text}\nLabel:"
    return prompt


# Negative-leaning query
query = "Completely useless, waste of money"
query_emb = np.array([0.05, 0.05, 0.85])  # similar to negative examples

prompt = build_dynamic_prompt(query, query_emb)
print("Dynamic prompt:")
print(prompt)
print("\n(Model would complete with: Negative)")
Structured Output and Format Control

A common motivation for adding few-shot examples is to enforce output format — the developer wants JSON, a specific label string, or a fixed template. In modern model APIs, structured output (JSON mode, tool use with schema) is a more reliable approach that does not consume tokens for examples:
Key Takeaways
Zero-shot
asks the model to perform a task with no examples — lowest token cost, fastest to deploy.
Few-shot
includes 1–8 input-output examples to anchor format, label boundaries, and tone.
Start zero-shot, measure accuracy on a representative sample, add examples only if accuracy is below threshold.
A single well-chosen example often provides most of the benefit of three to five examples.
Token cost for few-shot scales linearly — evaluate the cost-accuracy tradeoff at your request volume.
Use structured output APIs to fix format issues instead of wasting examples on formatting.
Escalate to fine-tuning when prompts need 10+ examples or inference cost becomes unacceptable.
Sanitize user inputs in few-shot prompts to prevent prompt injection.

The choice between zero-shot and few-shot is primarily a trade-off between token cost and output reliability. Zero-shot is cheaper and faster; few-shot is more reliable on tasks where the desired format or label boundary is hard to specify in words. The question is not which is better in the abstract — it is which is necessary for your specific task. Most tasks with well-known names (translation, summarisation, classification into standard categories) work well zero-shot with modern instruction-tuned models. Tasks with custom output formats, niche domain conventions, or ambiguous label definitions benefit from examples.
Side-by-Side Comparison

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def ask(prompt: str, stop: list[str] | None = None) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=20,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=stop or [],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


TEST_INPUTS = [
    "The shipment arrived three days late and the packaging was crushed.",
    "Exactly what I ordered — fast shipping too.",
    "It works but the manual is confusing.",
]

# Zero-shot
def classify_zero(text: str) -> str:
    return ask(
        f"Classify as Positive, Negative, or Neutral.\nText: {text}\nLabel:",
        stop=["\n"],
    )


# Few-shot — three examples anchor the label boundary
FEW_SHOT_EXAMPLES = """Text: Great product, fast delivery.
Label: Positive

Text: Broken on arrival, no response from support.
Label: Negative

Text: Does what it says. Nothing special.
Label: Neutral

"""

def classify_few(text: str) -> str:
    return ask(
        f"{FEW_SHOT_EXAMPLES}Text: {text}\nLabel:",
        stop=["\n"],
    )


print(f"{'Input':<55} {'Zero-shot':>12} {'Few-shot':>10}")
print("-" * 80)
for text in TEST_INPUTS:
    z = classify_zero(text)
    f = classify_few(text)
    agreement = "✓" if z == f else "✗"
    print(f"{text[:54]:<55} {z:>12} {f:>10}  {agreement}")
Decision Framework
Signal
Use zero-shot
Use few-shot
Task name familiarity
Standard name ("translate", "summarise")
Custom or domain-specific task
Output format
Standard format (JSON, prose)
Custom schema or proprietary format
Label ambiguity
Clear, universally understood categories
Overlapping or nuanced categories
Latency / cost
Priority — fewer tokens
Accuracy matters more than token cost
Zero-shot accuracy
Meets the bar in testing
Below bar — examples likely to help
Token Cost of Few-shot

# Estimating the token overhead of few-shot examples
# (rough estimate: ~1.3 tokens per word for English)

def estimate_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    return int(len(text.split()) * 1.3)

ZERO_SHOT_PROMPT = "Classify as Positive, Negative, or Neutral.\nText: {input}\nLabel:"

ONE_SHOT_EXAMPLE = """
Text: Great product, fast delivery.
Label: Positive
"""

THREE_SHOT_EXAMPLES = ONE_SHOT_EXAMPLE * 3   # simplified — real examples differ

query = "The delivery was late but the item is fine."

zero_tokens = estimate_tokens(ZERO_SHOT_PROMPT.format(input=query))
one_tokens  = estimate_tokens(ONE_SHOT_EXAMPLE + ZERO_SHOT_PROMPT.format(input=query))
three_tokens = estimate_tokens(THREE_SHOT_EXAMPLES + ZERO_SHOT_PROMPT.format(input=query))

print(f"Zero-shot:   ~{zero_tokens} tokens")
print(f"One-shot:    ~{one_tokens} tokens  (+{one_tokens - zero_tokens})")  
print(f"Three-shot:  ~{three_tokens} tokens (+{three_tokens - zero_tokens})")

# At $0.25/M input tokens (Haiku pricing), 3-shot adds ~$0.000025 per call —
# negligible for a single call, but meaningful at 10M calls/month (~$250 extra).
# Evaluate whether accuracy gain justifies the overhead at your scale.

Start zero-shot, measure accuracy on a representative sample, and only add examples if the measured accuracy is below your threshold. This keeps prompts lean, reduces latency, and avoids cargo-culting few-shot examples that do not actually help. When you do add examples, start with one — a single well-chosen example often eliminates format drift and provides most of the benefit of three or five. Add more only if one is not enough, and measure after each addition.
